news_summary: Discoveries of ancient artifacts in America and Syria are shedding new light on early human culture and history.
news_summary_fr: Les découvertes d'objets anciens en Amérique et en Syrie apportent un éclairage nouveau sur la culture et l'histoire des premiers humains.
news_summary_es: Los hallazgos de artefactos antiguos en América y Siria están arrojando nueva luz sobre la cultura y la historia de los primeros seres humanos.

articles.title: Ancient bone dice reveal 12,000-year history of gambling in America
articles.summary: More than 12,000 years ago, Native American hunter-gatherers were already making and using dice—thousands of years before similar tools appeared elsewhere. These bone “binary lots” acted like primitive coins, producing random outcomes for games of chance. A new study shows these weren’t accidental objects but carefully designed tools used across many regions and cultures.
articles.content:
A new study in American Antiquity, a leading journal of North American archaeology published by Cambridge University Press for the Society for American Archaeology, presents compelling evidence that the earliest known dice were created and used by Native American hunter-gatherers more than 12,000 years ago. These discoveries come from the western Great Plains at the end of the last Ice Age and predate the oldest known dice from Bronze Age societies in the Old World by thousands of years.

Research led by Colorado State University Ph.D. student Robert J. Madden shows that dice, gambling, and games of chance have deep roots in Native American culture, stretching back at least 12,000 years. The earliest examples come from Late Pleistocene Folsom-period sites in Wyoming, Colorado, and New Mexico. These artifacts are more than 6,000 years older than comparable dice found in the Old World.

"Historians have traditionally treated dice and probability as Old World innovations," Madden said. "What the archaeological record shows is that ancient Native American groups were deliberately making objects designed to produce random outcomes, and using those outcomes in structured games, thousands of years earlier than previously recognized."

What Ice Age Dice Looked Like

The oldest specimens identified in the study date to roughly 12,800-12,200 years ago. Unlike modern six-sided dice, these objects were two-sided pieces known as "binary lots." They were carefully shaped from bone into small, handheld forms that were flat or slightly rounded, often oval or rectangular, and designed to be tossed together onto a surface.

Each piece had two distinct faces, marked by differences in color, texture, or added designs, similar to heads and tails on a coin. One side served as the "counting" face. When thrown, each piece would land showing one side or the other, producing a binary (two-outcome) result. Players cast multiple pieces at once, and the outcome depended on how many landed with the counting face up.

"They're simple, elegant tools," Madden said. "But they're also unmistakably purposeful. These are not casual byproducts of bone working. They were made to generate random outcomes."

A New Method to Identify Ancient Dice

To move beyond guesswork, the study introduces an attribute-based morphological test, a structured checklist of physical characteristics used to identify dice in archaeological collections. This method is based on a comparative analysis of 293 sets of historic Native American dice recorded by ethnographer Stewart Culin in his 1907 Bureau of American Ethnology monograph, Games of the North American Indians.

Using this framework, the study revisits artifacts that had previously been labeled as possible "gaming pieces" or ignored entirely. By applying consistent criteria, Madden was able to determine whether these objects fit the definition of dice.

In many cases, the items had been known for decades but were never evaluated within a broader pattern. With this new approach, the study identifies more than 600 diagnostic and probable dice from sites covering every major period of North American prehistory, from the Late Pleistocene through and after European contact.

"In most cases, these objects had already been excavated and published," Madden said. "What was missing wasn't the evidence, it was a clear, continent-wide standard for recognizing what we were looking at."

The earliest examples were also examined directly in museum collections at the Smithsonian Institution, the University of Wyoming Archaeological Repository, and the Denver Museum of Nature and Science.

Rethinking the Origins of Probability

Dice games are often considered humanity's earliest structured interaction with randomness, laying the groundwork for probability theory, statistics, and scientific reasoning. Until now, scholars believed these practices originated in complex Old World societies around 5,500 years ago.

The new findings point to a much earlier and more widespread origin.

"These findings don't claim that Ice Age hunter-gatherers were doing formal probability theory," Madden said. "But they were intentionally creating, observing, and relying on random outcomes in repeatable, rule-based ways that leveraged probabilistic regularities, such as the law of large numbers. That matters for how we understand the global history of probabilistic thinking."

A Long-Lasting Cultural Tradition

The research also highlights how widespread and enduring dice games have been in Native American cultures. Evidence of dice appears at 57 archaeological sites across a 12-state region, spanning Paleoindian, Archaic, and Late Prehistoric periods, and reflecting a wide range of cultural traditions and lifestyles.

Madden suggests that this long history points to the important social role of games of chance. "Games of chance and gambling created neutral, rule-governed spaces for ancient Native Americans," he said. "They allowed people from different groups to interact, exchange goods and information, form alliances, and manage uncertainty. In that sense, they functioned as powerful social technologies."

About the Study

The article, "Probability in the Pleistocene: Origins and Antiquity of Native American Dice, Games of Chance, and Gambling," will appear in American Antiquity, published by Cambridge University Press on behalf of the Society for American Archaeology.
articles.reasoning: The article reveals a significant archaeological discovery that reshapes understanding of the origins of dice and games of chance, showing deep cultural roots in Native American history over 12,000 years ago. This finding has broad significance for history, anthropology, and the understanding of human cultural development, highlighting a meaningful and well-documented breakthrough with wide scholarly and cultural impact.
articles.personality_title: Ancient Native American dice show 12,000-year history of games of chance
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Archaeologists have long thought that dice and gambling started around 5,500 years ago in complex societies in Europe and Asia. Dice are small objects used to create random results in games, helping people decide outcomes by chance.

**What happened** – A new study led by Robert J. Madden from Colorado State University found that Native American hunter-gatherers were making and using dice more than 12,000 years ago. These ancient dice were discovered in the western Great Plains of the United States, in places like Wyoming, Colorado, and New Mexico. Instead of six-sided dice like today, these were two-sided bone pieces called "binary lots," similar to coins with two different faces, tossed to produce random results.

**Impact** – This discovery pushes back the known origin of dice and gambling by thousands of years and shows that Native Americans were among the first people to create tools for games of chance. It changes how we understand the history of probability and gambling, showing that these ideas existed long before they appeared in Europe or Asia. It also reveals that these games had important social roles, helping ancient communities interact, trade, and build relationships.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying these ancient dice using a new method that checks physical features to identify dice in museum collections and archaeological sites. This approach has already found hundreds of dice across many periods in North America. Future work might uncover more about how these games were played and their cultural meanings.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Native American hunter-gatherers invented and used dice for games of chance over 12,000 years ago, revealing an ancient and widespread tradition of gambling in North America.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les dés anciens des Amérindiens révèlent 12 000 ans d’histoire du jeu de hasard
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les archéologues pensaient que les dés et les jeux de hasard avaient commencé il y a environ 5 500 ans dans des sociétés complexes en Europe et en Asie. Les dés sont de petits objets utilisés pour créer des résultats aléatoires dans les jeux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude dirigée par Robert J. Madden de l’Université d’État du Colorado a montré que les chasseurs-cueilleurs amérindiens fabriquaient et utilisaient des dés il y a plus de 12 000 ans. Ces dés anciens ont été découverts dans les Grandes Plaines de l’Ouest américain, notamment au Wyoming, Colorado et Nouveau-Mexique. Ces dés n’étaient pas à six faces comme aujourd’hui, mais des morceaux d’os à deux faces appelés « lots binaires », semblables à des pièces de monnaie lancées pour obtenir un résultat aléatoire.

**Impact** – Cette découverte repousse l’origine connue des dés et du jeu de hasard de plusieurs milliers d’années et montre que les Amérindiens ont été parmi les premiers à créer des outils pour les jeux de hasard. Elle change notre compréhension de l’histoire des probabilités et révèle que ces idées existaient bien avant l’Europe ou l’Asie. Elle montre aussi que ces jeux avaient un rôle social important pour les communautés anciennes.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier ces dés anciens avec une nouvelle méthode qui analyse leurs caractéristiques physiques pour identifier les dés dans les collections de musées et les sites archéologiques. Cette méthode a déjà permis de trouver des centaines de dés à travers différentes périodes en Amérique du Nord. Les recherches futures pourraient en apprendre plus sur la façon dont ces jeux étaient pratiqués et leur signification culturelle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les chasseurs-cueilleurs amérindiens ont inventé et utilisé des dés pour les jeux de hasard il y a plus de 12 000 ans, révélant une tradition ancienne et répandue du jeu en Amérique du Nord.
articles.personality_title_es: Antiguos dados nativos americanos revelan 12,000 años de historia de juegos de azar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los arqueólogos pensaban que los dados y los juegos de azar comenzaron hace unos 5,500 años en sociedades complejas de Europa y Asia. Los dados son pequeños objetos usados para crear resultados aleatorios en juegos.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio dirigido por Robert J. Madden de la Universidad Estatal de Colorado encontró que los cazadores-recolectores nativos americanos fabricaban y usaban dados hace más de 12,000 años. Estos dados antiguos se descubrieron en las Grandes Llanuras del oeste de Estados Unidos, en lugares como Wyoming, Colorado y Nuevo México. En lugar de dados de seis caras como los actuales, estos eran piezas óseas de dos caras llamadas "lotes binarios", similares a monedas con dos caras diferentes, que se lanzaban para obtener resultados aleatorios.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo adelanta el origen conocido de los dados y el juego de azar por miles de años y muestra que los nativos americanos fueron de los primeros en crear herramientas para juegos de azar. Cambia la forma en que entendemos la historia de la probabilidad y revela que estas ideas existían mucho antes que en Europa o Asia. También muestra que estos juegos tenían un papel social importante para las comunidades antiguas.

**Próximos pasos** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando estos dados antiguos usando un nuevo método que revisa las características físicas para identificar dados en colecciones de museos y sitios arqueológicos. Este método ya ha encontrado cientos de dados en diferentes períodos en Norteamérica. El trabajo futuro podría revelar más sobre cómo se jugaban y su significado cultural.

**Resumen en una oración** – Los cazadores-recolectores nativos americanos inventaron y usaron dados para juegos de azar hace más de 12,000 años, revelando una antigua y amplia tradición de juegos en América del Norte.
articles.image_prompt: Ancient, carefully carved bone dice with two distinct faces lie scattered on a warm, textured surface resembling weathered stone or earth, surrounded by subtle outlines of Ice Age-era bison and native plains grasses under a soft golden light, evoking the deep history and cultural significance of early Native American games of chance.

articles.title: Mysterious Greek inscription may reveal lost temple beneath Syria’s Great Mosque
articles.summary: A mysterious Greek inscription found beneath the Great Mosque of Homs could pinpoint the long-debated location of an ancient sun temple. Scholars now think the mosque sits atop a sacred site that transitioned from pagan worship to Christianity and then Islam. The find supports the idea that religious change in the region happened gradually, with overlapping beliefs rather than sudden shifts. It also reconnects the site to the powerful cult of Elagabalus, whose priest once became a Roman emperor.
articles.content:
A newly uncovered Greek inscription inside the Great Mosque of Homs in Syria is reviving a long-running debate about the location of the ancient Temple of the Sun. This temple was once tied to Elagabalus, a high priest who rose to become Roman emperor in the third century AD.

Homs, known in antiquity as Emesa, has long been recognized for its historical importance. At the center of the city stands the Great Mosque, a landmark famous for its unusual oval design and deep religious significance.

The inscription was found at the base of one of the mosque's columns during restoration work. The site itself has a complex past. It is associated with the 12th-century ruler Nur ad-Din and is believed to have been built over a church dedicated to St. John the Baptist. That church may have replaced an even earlier structure, possibly a pagan temple.

A recent study published in the archaeology journal Shedet revisits the question of whether the mosque stands on the remains of the Temple of Elagabalus. For decades, historians have debated this possibility but lacked clear evidence.

Dr. Maamoun Saleh Abdulkarim, a professor of archaeology and history at the University of Sharjah and the study's author, believes the inscription could be the missing piece. He suggests it may help trace the origins of a site that has served as a place of worship across multiple religions over time.

"This inscription, uncovered during restoration work, provides new evidence in a long-standing debate: Was the Temple of Elagabalus located beneath the current Great Mosque in the city center, or was it situated in the archaeological layers atop the tell (mound), where the remains of the Islamic Citadel of Homs now stand?" explains Prof. Abdulkarim.

Layers of Religion in Ancient Emesa

For years, researchers have examined texts, coins, and archaeological remains to determine whether the Great Mosque once functioned as a temple and later as a church. The newly studied inscription offers fresh insight into this enduring question.

Prof. Abdulkarim is analyzing the inscription to better understand how the current mosque may connect to earlier religious structures on the same site.

"If its association with solar cult symbolism is confirmed, it may indicate a spatial continuity between the pagan sanctuary and the later religious structures built on the same site," he said, adding, "Such evidence would significantly strengthen the argument that religious transformation in Emesa occurred through architectural layering and reinterpretation rather than a complete break. It also underscores the importance of interdisciplinary collaboration between archaeology, epigraphy, architectural history, and heritage conservation."

The study presents Emesa as a city shaped by three major religious phases: paganism, Christianity, and Islam. It highlights how these transitions unfolded over time, offering a detailed example of cultural continuity and adaptation in the Middle East.

The research "examines the evolution of religious life in Emesa during Roman and early Byzantine times. It illustrates how the city's religious landscape changed from the worship of local gods, such as Elagabalus, to the general acceptance of Christianity in the fourth century AD," notes Prof. Abdulkarim.

"Examining the political and social factors that led to this transformation reveals a complex history of religious tolerance and struggle that ultimately allowed Christianity to flourish."

Greek Inscription Details and Translation

The inscription is carved into a granite block that forms part of a column base inside the mosque. Written in Greek, the text is directly etched into the stone.

According to archaeologist Teriz Lyoun, head of the Excavation Department in Homs, the inscription had been buried beneath the mosque floor and was first uncovered during excavations in 2016.

The base of the column measures 1×1 m. The inscribed portion covers about 75 cm of the front, with the remaining 25 cm forming a decorative frame. "The writing in the inscription image appears symmetrical, formal, and spaced in horizontal lines, a style common in formal dedication or commemorative texts. The design is a multi-line inscription arranged in straight horizontal lines, and the plaque is framed by a decorative top border," Ms. Lyoun explained.

Because of years of unrest in Syria, the inscription was not fully studied right away. In May 2016, historian Abdulhadi Al-Najjar shared an early translation on Facebook. "The passage in the inscription is characterized by a heroic and militaristic tone, depicting a warrior ruler compared to the wind, the storm, and the leopard, defeating enemies and imposing tribute with fierce royal authority," he wrote.

The text describes a powerful ruler using vivid imagery, comparing him to natural forces and predatory animals. Prof. Abdulkarim notes that the Greek contains grammatical irregularities, which were common in Roman-era Syria, where Aramaic was the dominant spoken language.

His analysis suggests the inscription strengthens the proposed link between the mosque and an earlier pagan sanctuary.

"This Greek inscription, even if it is epic in nature and not very detailed, provides clues to the connection between this mosque and a pagan building, perhaps the Temple of the Sun, especially since researchers have long proposed this connection. Thus, every inscription from the Roman era that is uncovered in this mosque will add more knowledge to our understanding of the subject," Prof. Abdulkarim noted.

From Pagan Temple to Church to Mosque

Prof. Abdulkarim emphasizes the importance of Emesa as both a religious and commercial center. Located along key trade routes connecting Antioch, Damascus, and the broader Levant, the city played a major role in regional history.

"The Roman identity of Emesa was fundamentally based on paganism," Prof. Abdulkarim writes. The city's religious life centered on the sun god Elagabalus, whose temple served as a focal point for rituals and festivals.

Although the inscription had been noted before, it had not been thoroughly analyzed in academic research until now. The new study argues that it provides strong evidence for both the presence and location of the Temple of the Sun during the Roman period.

For nearly a century, scholars have debated where the temple stood. According to Prof. Abdulkarim, the new findings may finally clarify its location.

"My research helps clarify many longstanding questions and offers new insights into the religious transformation of the site across successive historical periods. It demonstrates that the site later became a church and was subsequently transformed into a mosque after the Islamic conquest, an evolution also documented in the writings of Arab historians," he said.

The study also explores how Emesa transitioned from a center of sun worship to an important hub of Christianity during Roman and early Byzantine times. The temple of Elagabalus played a central role in shaping the city's identity, influencing its politics, economy, and culture.

The Priest Who Became Roman Emperor

The religious elite associated with the Temple of the Sun held significant power. One of its high priests eventually became Roman emperor.

Before ascending to the throne in 218 AD, Elagabalus served as the chief priest of the Syrian sun god. After becoming emperor, he attempted to elevate this deity above all others across the Roman Empire.

Despite this effort, the shift from paganism to Christianity in Emesa was gradual rather than sudden.

"Christianity did not replace paganism abruptly. Instead, both communities coexisted for generations," Prof. Abdulkarim explains. He notes that religious change often unfolds over long periods, with overlapping traditions rather than clear breaks.

A City That Preserved Its Past

Beyond the inscription itself, the study highlights a broader pattern in Emesa's history. Rather than erasing earlier traditions, the city adapted and reinterpreted them.

"The transformation of Emesa was not a rupture; it was a negotiation between old beliefs and emerging faith, with religious change reshaping not only sacred buildings but also power dynamics, identity, and urban space."

The Temple of the Sun, dedicated to Elagabalus, remained central to the city's identity even as its function changed. Over time, the same site served as a temple, then a church, and finally a mosque.

Despite these shifts, it continued to stand at the heart of the city's religious and political life, reflecting a remarkable continuity across nearly two thousand years.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a Greek inscription beneath Syria's Great Mosque of Homs provides significant archaeological evidence clarifying the location of an ancient Temple of the Sun and illustrates a meaningful cultural and religious continuity over centuries. This finding enriches understanding of historical religious transformations in a key Middle Eastern city, offering broad educational and cultural benefits beyond a niche audience. The article is focused, detailed, and highlights interdisciplinary collaboration and heritage conservation, making it an inspiring story of historical preservation and knowledge.
articles.personality_title: Greek inscription under Syria’s Great Mosque reveals ancient sun temple site
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Great Mosque of Homs in Syria is built on a site with a long history. It has been a place of worship for different religions, including pagan sun worship, Christianity, and Islam. For many years, experts have debated if an ancient Temple of the Sun once stood there.

**What happened** – During restoration work, archaeologists found a Greek inscription carved into a stone at the base of a mosque column. This inscription, discovered in 2016 but only recently studied in detail, may prove that the ancient Temple of the Sun was located beneath the mosque. The text describes a powerful ruler and connects the site to the sun god worshipped by Elagabalus, a priest who became a Roman emperor.

**Impact** – This discovery provides strong evidence about how the site changed over time. It shows that the mosque was built on layered religious history rather than replacing earlier buildings completely. The finding helps historians understand that religious changes in the city happened slowly, with old and new beliefs overlapping. It also highlights the role of Emesa (ancient Homs) as a key cultural and religious center.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying the inscription and the site to learn more about the ancient temple and the city’s religious history. This work will involve archaeologists, historians, and conservators working together to preserve and explain this important place for future generations.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A Greek inscription found under the Great Mosque of Homs sheds new light on an ancient sun temple and reveals how the site’s religious history evolved over centuries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une inscription grecque sous la Grande Mosquée de Homs révèle l’emplacement d’un ancien temple du soleil
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Grande Mosquée de Homs en Syrie repose sur un site chargé d’histoire. Il a été un lieu de culte pour différentes religions, notamment le culte païen du soleil, le christianisme et l’islam. Depuis longtemps, les experts débattent de la présence d’un ancien Temple du Soleil à cet endroit.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors de travaux de restauration, des archéologues ont découvert une inscription grecque gravée dans une pierre à la base d’une colonne de la mosquée. Cette inscription, trouvée en 2016 mais étudiée en détail récemment, pourrait prouver que l’ancien Temple du Soleil se trouvait sous la mosquée. Le texte décrit un dirigeant puissant et relie le site au dieu solaire vénéré par Élagabal, un prêtre devenu empereur romain.

**Impact** – Cette découverte apporte une preuve importante sur la manière dont le site a évolué. Elle montre que la mosquée a été construite en superposant des histoires religieuses plutôt que de remplacer complètement les bâtiments précédents. Cette trouvaille aide les historiens à comprendre que les changements religieux dans la ville se sont faits lentement, avec des croyances anciennes et nouvelles qui coexistaient. Elle souligne aussi l’importance d’Emèse (l’ancienne Homs) comme centre culturel et religieux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont poursuivre l’étude de l’inscription et du site pour en apprendre davantage sur le temple ancien et l’histoire religieuse de la ville. Ce travail réunira archéologues, historiens et conservateurs pour préserver et expliquer ce lieu important aux générations futures.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une inscription grecque trouvée sous la Grande Mosquée de Homs éclaire un ancien temple du soleil et révèle l’évolution de l’histoire religieuse du site sur plusieurs siècles.
articles.personality_title_es: Inscripción griega bajo la Gran Mezquita de Homs revela sitio de antiguo templo solar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Gran Mezquita de Homs en Siria está construida sobre un lugar con una larga historia. Ha sido un sitio de culto para diferentes religiones, incluyendo el paganismo solar, el cristianismo y el islam. Durante años, los expertos han debatido si un antiguo Templo del Sol estuvo allí.

**Qué pasó** – Durante trabajos de restauración, arqueólogos encontraron una inscripción en griego grabada en una piedra en la base de una columna de la mezquita. Esta inscripción, descubierta en 2016 pero estudiada en detalle recientemente, podría probar que el antiguo Templo del Sol se encontraba debajo de la mezquita. El texto describe a un gobernante poderoso y conecta el lugar con el dios sol adorado por Elagábalo, un sacerdote que llegó a ser emperador romano.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo aporta evidencia importante sobre cómo el sitio cambió con el tiempo. Muestra que la mezquita fue construida sobre capas de historia religiosa en lugar de reemplazar completamente edificios anteriores. El descubrimiento ayuda a entender que los cambios religiosos en la ciudad fueron graduales, con creencias antiguas y nuevas coexistiendo. También destaca la importancia de Emesa (la antigua Homs) como centro cultural y religioso.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán estudiando la inscripción y el sitio para aprender más sobre el antiguo templo y la historia religiosa de la ciudad. Este trabajo involucrará a arqueólogos, historiadores y conservadores para preservar y explicar este lugar importante para futuras generaciones.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una inscripción griega encontrada bajo la Gran Mezquita de Homs revela un antiguo templo solar y muestra cómo evolucionó la historia religiosa del lugar durante siglos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient stone column base inscribed with Greek letters, partially uncovered beneath the floor of an oval-shaped mosque interior bathed in soft golden light, with layered translucent silhouettes of a sun temple’s radiant solar disk, a Byzantine church’s arched dome, and a mosque’s pointed minaret rising gently behind it, symbolizing centuries of religious transformation in the historic city of Emesa.
